question:
Prove that, for every set $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ of $n$
real numbers, there exists a non-empty subset $S$ of $X$ and an integer $m$
such that
\[
\left| m + \sum_{s \in S} s \right| \leq \frac{1}{n+1}.
\]
solution:
Let $\{x\} = x - \lfloor x \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $x$.
For $i=0,\dots, n$, put $s_i = x_1 + \cdots + x_i$ (so that $s_0 = 0$).
Sort the numbers $\{s_0\}, \dots, \{s_n\}$ into ascending order,
and call the result $t_0, \dots, t_n$. Since $0 = t_0 \leq \cdots \leq
t_n < 1$, the differences
\[
t_1 - t_0, \dots, t_n - t_{n-1}, 1 - t_n
\]
are nonnegative and add up to 1. Hence (as in  the pigeonhole principle) one
of these differences
is no more than $1/(n+1)$; if it is anything other than $1 - t_n$,
it equals $\pm (\{s_i\} - \{s_j\})$ for some
$0 \leq i < j \leq n$. Put $S = \{x_{i+1}, \dots, x_j\}$ and
$m = \lfloor s_i \rfloor - \lfloor s_j \rfloor$; then
\begin{align*}
\left| m + \sum_{s \in S} s \right|
&= |m + s_j - s_i| \\
&= |\{s_j\} - \{s_i\}| \\
&\leq \frac{1}{n+1},
\end{align*}
as desired. In case $1 - t_n \leq 1 / (n+1)$, we take
$S = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ and $m = -\lceil s_n \rceil$, and again obtain
the desired conclusion.

descriptive_long.question:
Prove that, for every set $setfull = \{elementa, x_2, \dots, elementn\}$ of $countsz$
real numbers, there exists a non-empty subset $subsetv$ of $setfull$ and an integer $integerm$
such that
\[
\left| integerm + \sum_{sumroot \in subsetv} sumroot \right| \leq \frac{1}{countsz+1}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Let $\{element\} = element - \lfloor element \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $element$.
For $iterator=0,\dots, countsz$, put $indexsum = elementa + \cdots + elementi$ (so that $sumzero = 0$).
Sort the numbers $\{sumzero\}, \dots, \{sumsmax\}$ into ascending order,
and call the result $ordzero, \dots, ordnmax$. Since $0 = ordzero \leq \cdots \leq
ordnmax < 1$, the differences
\[
baseord_1 - ordzero, \dots, ordnmax - baseord_{countsz-1}, 1 - ordnmax
\]
are nonnegative and add up to 1. Hence (as in  the pigeonhole principle) one
of these differences
is no more than $1/(countsz+1)$; if it is anything other than $1 - ordnmax$,
it equals $\pm (\{indexsum\} - \{sumroot_j\})$ for some
$0 \leq iterator < j \leq countsz$. Put $subsetv = \{element_{iterator+1}, \dots, element_j\}$ and
$integerm = \lfloor indexsum \rfloor - \lfloor sumroot_j \rfloor$; then
\begin{align*}
\left| integerm + \sum_{sumroot \in subsetv} sumroot \right|
&= |integerm + sumroot_j - indexsum| \\
&= |\{sumroot_j\} - \{indexsum\}| \\
&\leq \frac{1}{countsz+1},
\end{align*}
as desired. In case $1 - ordnmax \leq 1 / (countsz+1)$, we take
$subsetv = \{elementa, \dots, elementn\}$ and $integerm = -\lceil sumsmax \rceil$, and again obtain
the desired conclusion.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that, for every set $peppermint = \{tapestry, x_2, \dots, honeycomb\}$ of $evergreen$
real numbers, there exists a non-empty subset $marigold$ of $peppermint$ and an integer $zeppelin$
such that
\[
\left| zeppelin + \sum_{cucumber \in marigold} cucumber \right| \leq \frac{1}{evergreen+1}.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Let $\{rainstorm\} = rainstorm - \lfloor rainstorm \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $rainstorm$.
For $strawhat=0,\dots, evergreen$, put $butterfly = tapestry + \cdots + windchime$ (so that $clipboard = 0$).
Sort the numbers $\{clipboard\}, \dots, \{harmonica\}$ into ascending order,
and call the result $quagmire, \dots, lighthouse$. Since $0 = quagmire \leq \cdots \leq
lighthouse < 1$, the differences
\[
 t_1 - quagmire, \dots, t_n - t_{n-1}, 1 - lighthouse
\]
are nonnegative and add up to 1. Hence (as in  the pigeonhole principle) one
of these differences
is no more than $1/(evergreen+1)$; if it is anything other than $1 - lighthouse$,
it equals $\pm (\{butterfly\} - \{s_j\})$ for some
$0 \leq strawhat < j \leq evergreen$. Put $marigold = \{x_{strawhat+1}, \dots, x_j\}$ and
$zeppelin = \lfloor butterfly \rfloor - \lfloor s_j \rfloor$; then
\begin{align*}
\left| zeppelin + \sum_{cucumber \in marigold} cucumber \right|
&= |zeppelin + s_j - butterfly| \\
&= |\{s_j\} - \{butterfly\}| \\
&\leq \frac{1}{evergreen+1},
\end{align*}
as desired. In case $1 - lighthouse \leq 1 / (evergreen+1)$, we take
$marigold = \{tapestry, \dots, honeycomb\}$ and $zeppelin = -\lceil harmonica \rceil$, and again obtain
the desired conclusion.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that, for every set $sequence = \{complexfirst, complexes_2, \dots, complexfinal\}$ of $infinite$
real numbers, there exists a non-empty subset $supersetset$ of $sequence$ and an integer $irrational$
such that
\[
\left| irrational + \sum_{difference \in supersetset} difference \right| \leq \frac{1}{infinite+1}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Let $\{complexes\} = complexes - \lfloor complexes \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of complexes.
For $indexless=0,\dots, infinite$, put $differenceindex = complexfirst + \cdots + complexindex$ (so that $differencezero = 0$).
Sort the numbers $\{differencezero\}, \dots, \{differencefinal\}$ into ascending order,
and call the result unsortedzero, \dots, unsortedfinal. Since $0 = unsortedzero \leq \cdots \leq
unsortedfinal < 1$, the differences
\[
t_1 - unsortedzero, \dots, unsortedfinal - t_{infinite-1}, 1 - unsortedfinal
\]
are nonnegative and add up to 1. Hence (as in  the pigeonhole principle) one
of these differences
is no more than $1/(infinite+1)$; if it is anything other than $1 - unsortedfinal$,
it equals $\pm (\{differenceindex\} - \{s_j\})$ for some
$0 \leq indexless < j \leq infinite$. Put $supersetset = \{complexes_{indexless+1}, \dots, complexes_j\}$ and
$irrational = \lfloor differenceindex \rfloor - \lfloor s_j \rfloor$; then
\begin{align*}
\left| irrational + \sum_{difference \in supersetset} difference \right|
&= |irrational + s_j - differenceindex| \\
&= |\{s_j\} - \{differenceindex\}| \\
&\leq \frac{1}{infinite+1},
\end{align*}
as desired. In case $1 - unsortedfinal \leq 1 / (infinite+1)$, we take
$supersetset = \{complexfirst, \dots, complexfinal\}$ and $irrational = -\lceil differencefinal \rceil$, and again obtain
the desired conclusion.

garbled_string.question:
Prove that, for every set $drnqsavm = \{ucnokims, kzpravle_2, \dots, rdycehvm\}$ of $swbfojzt$
real numbers, there exists a non-empty subset $ghrpqsle$ of $drnqsavm$ and an integer $zbvynkte$
such that
\[
\left| zbvynkte + \sum_{lmqzthar \in ghrpqsle} lmqzthar \right| \leq \frac{1}{swbfojzt+1}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Let $\{kzpravle\} = kzpravle - \lfloor kzpravle \rfloor$ denote the fractional part of $kzpravle$.
For $widjpmqa = 0, \dots, swbfojzt$, put $jpkarnul = ucnokims + \cdots + hgtlfwaz$ (so that $uwmnfocs = 0$).
Sort the numbers $\{uwmnfocs\}, \dots, \{qdervzop\}$ into ascending order,
and call the result $rfstoeqa, \dots, voxkrtim$. Since $0 = rfstoeqa \leq \cdots \leq
voxkrtim < 1$, the differences
\[
yxplomiv_1 - rfstoeqa, \dots, voxkrtim - yxplomiv_{swbfojzt-1}, 1 - voxkrtim
\]
are nonnegative and add up to 1. Hence (as in the pigeonhole principle) one
of these differences
is no more than $1/(swbfojzt+1)$; if it is anything other than $1 - voxkrtim$,
it equals $\pm (\{jpkarnul\} - \{\lmqzthar_j\})$ for some
$0 \leq widjpmqa < j \leq swbfojzt$. Put $ghrpqsle = \{kzpravle_{widjpmqa+1}, \dots, kzpravle_j\}$ and
$zbvynkte = \lfloor jpkarnul \rfloor - \lfloor \lmqzthar_j \rfloor$; then
\begin{align*}
\left| zbvynkte + \sum_{lmqzthar \in ghrpqsle} lmqzthar \right|
&= |zbvynkte + \lmqzthar_j - jpkarnul| \\
&= |\{\lmqzthar_j\} - \{jpkarnul\}| \\
&\leq \frac{1}{swbfojzt+1},
\end{align*}
as desired. In case $1 - voxkrtim \leq 1 / (swbfojzt+1)$, we take
$ghrpqsle = \{ucnokims, \dots, rdycehvm\}$ and $zbvynkte = -\lceil qdervzop \rceil$, and again obtain
the desired conclusion.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a positive integer and let x_1,\ldots ,x_n be real numbers.  Show that there exist indices 0\le p<q\le n (so that the sum x_{p+1}+\cdots +x_q is taken over a consecutive block of terms) and an integer k such that
\[
\left|\,k-\bigl(x_{p+1}+\cdots +x_q\bigr)\right|\le \frac1{n+1} .
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Let n\geq 1 and x_1,\ldots ,x_n be real. Define the partial sums
  s_0=0,   s_i=x_1+\cdots +x_i  for 1\leq i\leq n.
Write the fractional part {y}=y-\lfloor y\rfloor \in [0,1). Consider the n+1 numbers
  {s_0}, {s_1}, \ldots , {s_n}.
Since {s_0}=0, sort these into ascending order
  t_0\leq t_1\leq \cdots \leq t_n<1,
where t_0={s_0}=0. There are n+1 gaps
  t_1-t_0,  t_2-t_1, \ldots , t_n-t_{n-1}, 1-t_n,
and they sum to 1. Hence at least one gap \leq 1/(n+1). We distinguish two cases:

Case 1 (interior gap). Suppose for some 1\leq r\leq n the gap t_r-t_{r-1} \leq  1/(n+1). By definition there are 0\leq i<j\leq n with
  t_{r-1}={s_i}, t_r={s_j}.
Then
  0\leq {s_j}-{s_i}=t_r-t_{r-1}\leq 1/(n+1).
Set
  p=i, q=j, m=\lfloor s_i\rfloor -\lfloor s_j\rfloor \in \mathbb{Z}.
Then x_{p+1}+\cdots +x_q=s_j-s_i and
  m+(s_j-s_i)=\lfloor s_i\rfloor -\lfloor s_j\rfloor +s_j-s_i={s_j}-{s_i},
so
  |m+(x_{p+1}+\cdots +x_q)|=|{s_j}-{s_i}|\leq 1/(n+1).

Case 2 (wrap-around gap). Suppose instead
  1-t_n\leq 1/(n+1).
Then t_n={s_j} for some j (1\leq j\leq n), and
  1-{s_j}\leq 1/(n+1).
Take the block x_1,\ldots ,x_j so that p=0, q=j, and set
  m=-\lceil s_j\rceil \in \mathbb{Z}.
Then x_1+\cdots +x_j=s_j and
  m+s_j=-\lceil s_j\rceil +s_j=-(\lceil s_j\rceil -s_j)=-(1-{s_j}),
so
  |m+(x_1+\cdots +x_j)|=1-{s_j}\leq 1/(n+1).

In either case we have found 0\leq p<q\leq n and an integer m with
  |m+(x_{p+1}+\cdots +x_q)|\leq 1/(n+1).
Taking k=-m (also an integer) yields
  |k-(x_{p+1}+\cdots +x_q)|\leq 1/(n+1),
completing the proof.

core_steps: Form the partial sums s_i = x_1 + … + x_i with s_0 = 0
Take the fractional parts {s_i} and sort them to get t_0,…,t_n
Apply the pigeonhole principle to the n+1 gaps (t_1−t_0,…,t_n−t_{n−1},1−t_n) to obtain a gap ≤ 1/(n+1)
Express that gap as ±({s_j}−{s_i}) = m + (x_{i+1}+…+x_j) for a suitable integer m, giving the required bound

slot1.description: The order in which the fractional parts are arranged—any total order (e.g. descending) would work if gaps are taken consecutively on the circle.

slot2.description: The specific integer-part operator used; ceiling could replace floor throughout with sign adjustments.
slot2.original: floor function ⌊·⌋ in {x}=x−⌊x⌋ and in the definition m = ⌊s_i⌋−⌊s_j⌋

slot3.description: The choice of the ‘wrap-around’ gap 1−t_n; one could instead view the fractional parts on a circle and pick any of the n+1 cyclic gaps.
slot3.original: the additional interval 1 − t_n appended to the list of n interior gaps
